name: long-idle-live-session
description: Claude session enters ready, sits idle past readyTTL, and resumes working on the next user prompt. The recording proves irrlicht's PID-liveness check keeps the session alive past the TTL cutoff (the session would otherwise be swept on idle). Note: the production readyTTL is 30 min; this recording uses IRRLICHT_READY_SESSION_TTL=15s on the daemon so the assertion 'session stayed past readyTTL' is testable in ~45s instead of 32min. The shortened TTL only affects the recording — production behavior is unchanged.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

The scenario asserts three user-observable signals: (1) the session row persists in `ready` past `readyTTL`, (2) it is NOT pruned during the idle window because its process is still alive, and (3) the next user prompt resumes `ready → working` normally. The primitive exercised is the daemon's process-liveness check — `kill(pid, 0)` against the session's PID to distinguish "idle but alive" from "dead and eligible for pruning." This is agent-agnostic daemon logic that applies to any adapter with a discoverable, persistent PID. The discriminator across the matrix row is therefore whether the adapter has a real PID binding: claudecode/codex/pi land at `full`, opencode is the contrast because its ProcessOwnedStore over SQLite has no persistent PID. Aider patterns with the former group.

**agent_supports.** Aider declares `headless_mode: true` (capabilities.json), satisfying the scenario's `requires: [headless_mode]`. More importantly for this scenario, aider runs as a long-lived interactive REPL. The recording's `drive-aider-interactive.sh` launches a single aider TUI under `tmux new-session` (NOT the single-shot `--message`/`--print` path) and keeps that one process alive across both `send`s and the idle `sleep` between them — the tmux session is only killed at the very end of the step script. So the aider process is genuinely alive throughout the idle window, which is what the scenario requires.

**daemon_capability (full).** Aider's transport is `FilesUnderCWD` over the per-project `.aider.chat.history.md`, with a real PID binding: `DiscoverPID` matches command lines containing `/aider` whose CWD equals the session's working directory (the OS process is `python` invoking the aider script, so a bare `pgrep -x aider` finds nothing — the `/aider` command-line regex is what binds the PID). With that PID bound, the daemon's sweep at `pid_manager.go` first filters to stale sessions via `IsStale(readyTTL)`, then explicitly skips deletion when `state.PID > 0 && syscall.Kill(state.PID, 0) == nil` — i.e. the live aider process keeps the `ready` session out of the readyTTL sweep, exactly as it does for claudecode. No `transcript_removed` fires during the idle window. A trace exists in the watched Source and the daemon handles it correctly today, so this is `full`, not `bug`/`incapable`.

The parser emits the lifecycle events the spec asserts. Turn 1 lands in `ready` via `IdleFlush`, which synthesizes a `turn_done` once `.aider.chat.history.md` has been quiet for `aiderIdleTurnDoneAfter` (1500ms) — aider's idle TUI prompt never lands in the markdown history, so this synthetic flush is the canonical working→ready signal. The second `send` writes a `#### ` line, which the parser maps to `user_message` (reopening the turn → `working`); the subsequent `> Tokens:`/IdleFlush pair settles back to `ready`. The recipe's `tool_calls_max: 0` holds because both prompts are pure text replies with no `Applied edit`/`Running` lines.

The recording shortens the TTL via `IRRLICHT_READY_SESSION_TTL=15s` so the "stayed past readyTTL" assertion is testable in ~45s rather than 30min; this only affects the recording, not production behavior.

**driver_capability (ready).** The recipe needs three step types — `send`, `wait_turn`, and `sleep`. All three are implemented by `drive-aider-interactive.sh` (its `case` dispatches `send|slash`, `wait_turn`, `interrupt`, `sleep`). The long-idle scenario keeps the session alive and quiet, so the driver needs no `restart`/`sigkill`/`session`/`keys` primitive — only send + sleep, both present. No driver gap.

## Matrix drift

The coverage row currently reads `agent_supports: yes / daemon_capability: unknown / driver_capability: ready` for aider. This assessment resolves the `unknown` to `daemon_capability: full` — the `unknown` predated confirming that aider's `/aider` PID binding feeds the same liveness path (`pid_manager.go` kill(pid,0) skip) the other PID-backed adapters use. The maintainer should transcribe `daemon_capability: full` (note: "PID liveness via /aider command-line match holds session past readyTTL — same path as claudecode"). `driver_capability` stays `ready`.
caveats: Aider's idle-flush window (~1500ms parser floor, ~5s effective once the SessionDetector's 5s working-refresh tick is included) means the post-turn ready transition lags actual LLM completion; the recipe budgets sleeps around each turn to let the classifier settle.
Recording exercises a ~25s idle window against a 15s readyTTL; production sessions sit idle for hours. The assertion is a floor (stayed past TTL), so longer real-world idles still pass.

sources.note: Lines ~634-642: IsStale(readyTTL) filter then the kill(pid,0)==nil skip — the process-liveness check this scenario exercises; agent-agnostic, applies to any PID-bound adapter. Grounds daemon_capability: full (trace handled today).
FilesUnderCWD over .aider.chat.history.md; CommandPattern matches /aider so a persistent PID is discoverable.
DiscoverPID binds the aider PID by /aider command line + CWD — the binding the liveness check polls.
IdleFlush synthesizes turn_done (working→ready) after 1500ms quiet; #### lines map to user_message (ready→working) for the second turn.
headless_mode: true — the Primitive-exercised key; satisfies the scenario's requires: [headless_mode].

Launches ONE aider TUI via tmux and keeps it alive across both sends and the idle sleep; case dispatch implements send|slash, wait_turn, interrupt, sleep — the recipe's send+wait_turn+sleep are all present, so driver_capability: ready.
Contrast: opencode's ProcessOwnedStore has no persistent PID; aider's PID binding is why its daemon_capability lands full.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or OPENAI_API_KEY)
irrlichd is running with IRRLICHT_READY_SESSION_TTL=15s in its environment (the daemon's default 30 min would force a 32+ min recording); the shortened TTL only affects the recording, not production
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a --record daemon)
setup: Create an empty cwd under <staging>/cwd/ (drive-aider-interactive.sh git-inits it)

Aider's TUI launched via drive-aider-interactive.sh stays ONE live process across both sends and the idle sleep — the tmux session is killed only at the end, so the aider PID is genuinely alive throughout the idle window (that liveness is what keeps the session out of the readyTTL sweep)

Daemon's effective readyTTL is 15 s; the idle sleep (25 s) is calibrated to clearly exceed it even after aider's ~5 s idle-flush settle eats into the front of the window — so the clean ready dwell is ~20 s, well past the 15 s TTL

script.text: Answer in chat only as plain prose — do NOT create, edit, or modify any file, and do NOT output a SEARCH/REPLACE block. In one short sentence: what is the capital of France?
Answer in chat only as plain prose — do NOT create, edit, or modify any file, and do NOT output a SEARCH/REPLACE block. In one short sentence: what is the capital of Japan?

verify: events.jsonl shows aider entering ready after turn 1 completes (IdleFlush synthesizes the working→ready settle; effective latency ~5 s)
no transcript_removed event fires for the live aider session during the idle window (the session must NOT be swept past readyTTL while the aider process is alive)
no state_transition to working or waiting fires during the idle window (state stays ready while idle)
after the second send, events.jsonl shows ready → working → ready for the same live session (the long-idle session resumes normally)
final session state is ready
recipe.scope_note: Proves aider's PID-liveness keeps a ready session alive PAST readyTTL: aider's PID is discoverable via the /aider command-line match, so the daemon's sweep skips deletion while kill(pid,0)==nil — exactly as for claudecode. Recording uses IRRLICHT_READY_SESSION_TTL=15s so the 'past TTL' assertion is testable in ~45 s. Aider's idle-flush window (~5 s) means the post-turn ready transition lags actual LLM completion; the recipe budgets a 25 s idle sleep (≥20 s, clearly past the 15 s TTL) and a 12 s trailing sleep (gpt-5-nano is fast + aider's ~5 s settle) so each per-turn working→ready settles cleanly and the dwell clearly exceeds the TTL. PROMPT DESIGN (resolved drift): both prompts are PURE-CONVERSATIONAL factual asks (capital of France / Japan) that explicitly forbid file creation/edits and SEARCH/REPLACE blocks — same forbid-edits shape as aider/turn-end-terminal-text. This avoids the earlier 4/7 drift where 'Reply with exactly the word: ok' was non-deterministically interpreted by gpt-5-nano as a file-edit request, producing a post-turn `Applied edit to notes/ok.txt` write that landed ~8 s into the idle dwell and flicked ready→working, failing idle_window. With no file edit, the transcript goes quiet during the dwell so the ≥15 s held-ready window holds.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

The spec asserts three observables: (1) the session does NOT disappear past readyTTL, (2) state remains `ready` during idle, and (3) the next user prompt resumes `ready → working` normally. All three are within claudecode + irrlicht's transport.

Claude Code is a long-lived TUI process — once launched, it sits in its REPL until the user explicitly exits. irrlicht's adapter ties session lifetime to PID liveness (`ExactName{Name: "claude"}` via pgrep) rather than to a fixed timer. The classifier's `readyTTL` is the timeout for ready-state STALE sessions (no live process backing them); for sessions whose PID is still alive, the session stays in `ready` indefinitely. The `idle_window` phase in the spec asserts this with `duration_at_least_ms: 15000` plus invariants (no transcript_removed, no spurious state_transition to working/waiting), and the matrix notes for this cell already cite '#321 zombie-on-restart fixed in 0.4.x' — the PID liveness path is exercised by the current daemon.

The second-turn behaviour is the normal turn arc: a fresh `send` after the idle window drives state to `working` via the JSONL transcript's user-line append; the assistant's response settles back to `ready`. The recipe's 20-second sleep between turns clears any reasonable readyTTL.
caveats: Recording exercises a 15-20 s idle window; production sessions sit idle for hours. The phase contract uses duration_at_least_ms (not _at_most_ms), so longer real-world idles are by definition still passing. The phase budget is the floor, not the ceiling.

If a future daemon version reintroduces a session-level idle timer (separate from PID liveness), the idle_window invariants would fire and this spec would catch the regression. That's the test's intent.

sources.note: ExactName{Name: "claude"} process matcher — session lifetime is tied to PID liveness, not a timer.
by_adapter.claudecode.script for long-idle-live-session — 6 steps: send + wait + 20s sleep + send + wait + 5s tail.
Zombie-on-restart fix in 0.4.x exercises the PID-liveness path this scenario depends on.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is running with IRRLICHT_READY_SESSION_TTL=15s in its environment (the daemon's default 30 min would force a 32+ min recording)
no other claudecode session uses the recipe's UUID
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd (claude prompts for trust on first encounter)

Daemon's effective readyTTL must be < 30 s so the post-turn sleep can clearly exceed it. The driver script's first sleep (20 s) is calibrated to 1.33× a 15 s TTL — long enough to prove 'past TTL' without burning a full minute

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
Reply with exactly the word: alive

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready after turn 1 completes (state_transition to ready)
no transcript_removed event fires for the UUID session during the 20 s idle window (the session must not be swept while the claude process is alive)
no state_transition to working or waiting fires during the 20 s idle window (state stays ready while idle)
after the second send, events.jsonl shows ready → working → ready for the same UUID (the long-idle session resumes normally)
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains exactly 2 user lines and 2 assistant lines

metadata.notes: Codex's REPL is a persistent process; the daemon's PID-liveness check holds the rollout session across the recipe's 20s idle window. Recording shows ready→working→ready before the idle window and ready→working→ready after the second send, with no transcript_removed event in between.

assessment.body: Codex's REPL is a persistent process; the daemon's PID-liveness check holds the rollout session across the recipe's 20s idle window. Recording shows ready→working→ready before the idle window and ready→working→ready after the second send, with no transcript_removed event in between.
caveats: Production daemon readyTTL is 30 min; this recording proves continuity over 20 s but does not exercise the TTL itself. IRRLICHT_READY_SESSION_TTL=15s on the daemon would harden the assertion.

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.codex/config.toml or OPENAI_API_KEY)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a --record daemon)
no other codex session uses the recipe's UUID
setup: Codex's REPL launched via drive-codex-interactive.sh stays alive across the recipe's two sends, so the daemon's process scanner observes one continuous PID across the 20 s idle window

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
Reply with exactly the word: alive

verify: events.jsonl shows the codex rollout session entering ready after turn 1
no transcript_removed event fires for the rollout session during the 20 s idle window
after the second send, events.jsonl shows ready → working → ready for the same rollout session
final session state is ready
recipe.scope_note: Tests that codex's rollout-keyed session survives a 20 s idle window between turns. The daemon's production readyTTL is 30 min, so this recording validates 'session stays observed during idle' rather than 'past TTL' — that stricter assertion would require IRRLICHT_READY_SESSION_TTL=15s on the daemon.

metadata.notes: OpenCode's `opencode run --session <id>` spawns a fresh process per turn but reuses the SQLite session row. The daemon observes one continuous ses_… id across the 20s idle window even though the underlying process exits and is re-launched. Recording validates 4/4 phases (session birth, turn1 cycle, session re-emergence after idle); the second turn's working-state transition isn't always captured because opencode's per-turn process can complete inside a single classifier tick.

assessment.body: OpenCode's `opencode run --session <id>` spawns a fresh process per turn but reuses the SQLite session row. The daemon observes one continuous ses_… id across the 20s idle window even though the underlying process exits and is re-launched. Recording validates 4/4 phases (session birth, turn1 cycle, session re-emergence after idle); the second turn's working-state transition isn't always captured because opencode's per-turn process can complete inside a single classifier tick.
caveats: No persistent PID across turns: irrlicht's PID-liveness check doesn't apply; session continuity comes from the SQLite session table.
Second turn may complete too quickly to observe a ready→working transition.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled
setup: drive-opencode-interactive.sh issues two `opencode run --session <id>` invocations sharing the same session id, so the SQLite-backed session row spans the idle window

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
Reply with exactly the word: alive

verify: events.jsonl shows the opencode ses_… session entering ready after turn 1
the same ses_… id is observed across both turns (single SQLite session row, not two)
after the second send, events.jsonl shows ready → working → ready for the same ses_… id
final session state is ready
recipe.scope_note: OpenCode's process model is one `opencode run` invocation per turn (no persistent REPL); the SQLite session row chains them via --session. The daemon observes the session via SQLite polling rather than a continuous PID.

metadata.notes: Pi's REPL (driven via tmux) is a persistent process; the daemon's PID-liveness check holds the UUID-keyed session across the 20s idle window. Recording shows ready→working→ready before idle and ready→working→ready after the second send.

assessment.body: Pi's REPL (driven via tmux) is a persistent process; the daemon's PID-liveness check holds the UUID-keyed session across the 20s idle window. Recording shows ready→working→ready before idle and ready→working→ready after the second send.
caveats: Production daemon readyTTL is 30 min; this recording proves session continuity over 20s but does not exercise the TTL itself.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (PI_API_KEY)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a --record daemon)
no concurrent pi REPL is running in the user's other terminal windows (the interactive driver assumes pi can take exclusive control)
setup: Pi's REPL launched via drive-pi-interactive.sh stays alive across the recipe's two sends
